en.mechanism_prose: Both substances suppress the brainstem reflexes that drive breathing. Combined, the effect compounds — respiratory depression that either alone might not cause becomes lethal. Sedation also stacks, so the person stops noticing they are in trouble before breathing fails. This is the most common cause of accidental overdose deaths in the US and Europe.
warning_signs: Blue or gray lips, fingertips, or skin
first_aid: Call 911 / 112 immediately — do not wait to see if it gets better
Place them in recovery position (on their side, top knee bent, head tilted)
If naloxone (Narcan) is available, give it; repeat every 2-3 minutes if no response
Stay with them and rescue breathe if trained — call 911 back if they stop breathing

en.mechanism_prose: Stacking two depressants compounds central nervous system suppression. The body slows breathing, heart rate, and gag reflex in ways that either substance alone would not. The risk window is also longer than people expect: an extra drink hours after another sedative can tip a manageable state into unconsciousness and aspiration. Vomiting while unconscious is a common cause of death here.
first_aid: Call 911 / 112 immediately — do not assume they will sleep it off
Place them in recovery position so vomit can drain, not block the airway
Stay with them and watch breathing; do not give coffee or cold showers
Keep calling 911 if breathing stops — start rescue breaths if trained

en.mechanism_prose: A depressant lowers breathing and protective reflexes; a dissociative removes body awareness and motor control. Together they can knock someone unconscious with little warning and stop them clearing their own airway. The biggest killers are aspirating vomit and respiratory depression. Cold water, baths, and pools are especially dangerous because the person cannot judge their position or react.
warning_signs: Passing out or becoming impossible to wake
first_aid: Move them away from water, stairs, and open windows
Place in recovery position so they cannot aspirate
Stay with them; do not let them bathe, shower, or swim
Call 911 / 112 now if unresponsive, breathing changes, or you cannot wake them

en.mechanism_prose: Even at lower intensity, a depressant plus a dissociative impairs balance, judgement, and the ability to swallow safely. People fall, choke, or pass out in unsafe places. The dissociative hides how sedated the depressant has made them, so they keep moving, driving, or dosing when they should stop.
warning_signs: Stumbling, falls, or inability to stand
first_aid: Get them somewhere flat and safe, away from water and stairs
Keep them on their side if they lie down
Do not give more of either substance
Call 911 / 112 if they cannot be roused or breathing slows

en.mechanism_prose: A depressant and a dissociative both dull coordination and awareness. At cautious doses the main risks are falls, nausea, and misjudging how impaired you are. The dissociative can mask the depressant's sedation, making it easy to take more than intended.
warning_signs: Feeling more sober than you actually are
first_aid: Sit or lie down somewhere safe and stop dosing
Hydrate slowly with water
Have a sober friend keep an eye on you
If drowsiness deepens or breathing slows, call 911 / 112

en.mechanism_prose: Stimulants mask the sedating effect of depressants, so people take more of both than they otherwise would. Cocaine plus alcohol also forms cocaethylene in the liver — a longer-lasting metabolite that is harder on the heart than cocaine alone. When the stimulant fades first, the depressant can hit suddenly and dangerously.
warning_signs: Drinking or dosing far more than usual without feeling it
Chest pain or a pounding, racing heart
Sudden heavy sedation as the stimulant wears off
first_aid: Stop using and rest somewhere cool and calm
Sip water; avoid more caffeine or stimulants
Watch the heart and breathing as the stimulant fades
Chest pain or trouble breathing means call 911 / 112 now

en.mechanism_prose: Stimulants hide how intoxicated a depressant is making you. The classic example is alcohol with MDMA or another stimulant: you feel alert, so you drink more, then the stimulant wears off and the alcohol lands all at once. The cardiovascular system is also working harder than it would on either alone.
warning_signs: Not feeling as drunk or sedated as expected
Crashing hard when the stimulant fades
first_aid: Pace yourself and switch to water
Cool down and rest if overheating
Plan for a heavier comedown as the stimulant fades
If chest pain or fainting occurs, call 911 / 112

en.mechanism_prose: Two stimulants stack the load on the heart and on the body's heat regulation. Heart rate, blood pressure, and temperature climb together. The user feels alert and capable, but the cardiovascular strain is invisible from the inside. Dehydration and overheating are the most common harms; cardiac events are rarer but can happen when stimulant doses stack.
warning_signs: Chest pain, pressure, or pounding heartbeat
Hot, dry skin instead of sweating
Jaw clenching or tremor that will not settle
first_aid: Move to a cool place and stop dancing or exerting
Sip water slowly to rehydrate
Do not redose — the next dose stacks the load
Chest pain, trouble breathing, or confusion means call 911 / 112

en.mechanism_prose: The stimulant masks dissociative effects, making it easy to redose the dissociative to risky levels. Stimulant-driven hyperthermia and cardiovascular load combine with dissociation-driven loss of body awareness — the person feels alert but cannot judge their own state. Hospital visits for this mix cluster around redose accidents and overheating, not direct toxicity.
warning_signs: Hot skin or rising body temperature
Rapid heartbeat that will not settle
first_aid: Move to a cool, quiet space and reduce stimulation
Hydrate slowly with water, not energy drinks
Do not give more of either substance
If unresponsive or breathing changes, call 911 / 112

en.mechanism_prose: With stronger dissociatives like PCP, the stimulant's masking of dissociation becomes dangerous. People misjudge dose badly, become agitated or unaware of their surroundings, and overheat. Coordination and judgement drop while the stimulant pushes the heart and temperature up.
first_aid: Reduce noise, light, and stimulation; keep them and others safe
Cool them down and offer water if alert enough to drink
Do not redose; do not restrain forcefully
If overheating, chest pain, or unresponsiveness occurs, call 911 / 112

en.mechanism_prose: Opioids suppress breathing; dissociatives remove the body awareness that would normally make someone notice they are in trouble. Many dissociatives (and tramadol itself) also lower the seizure threshold. The combination can stop breathing while the person is too dissociated to react, and can trigger seizures.
first_aid: Call 911 / 112 immediately
If naloxone (Narcan) is available, give it
Place in recovery position; protect the head if seizing
Stay and rescue breathe if trained — keep 911 on the line if breathing stops

en.mechanism_prose: Stimulants mask an opioid's sedation, so people take more opioid than their breathing can handle; when the stimulant fades first, breathing can crash. Tramadol adds two more dangers: it lowers the seizure threshold, and with serotonergic stimulants like MDMA it can trigger serotonin syndrome. The heart is also under heavy strain.
warning_signs: Slow or stopped breathing, especially as the stimulant fades
first_aid: Call 911 / 112 immediately
If naloxone is available, give it; protect the head if seizing
Move to a cool place; do not give more of anything
Recovery position if unresponsive — stay until help arrives, keep calling 911

en.mechanism_prose: Tramadol and some other opioids lower the seizure threshold and add serotonergic load; classic psychedelics add their own serotonin activity and can amplify anxiety. The combination raises seizure risk and can make a difficult headspace much harder to manage, while the opioid still suppresses breathing.
warning_signs: Twitching, muscle jerks, or a seizure
first_aid: Move to a calm, safe space and reduce stimulation
Protect the head and clear the area if a seizure happens
Do not give more substances
Seizure, very high temperature, or slowed breathing means call 911 / 112

en.mechanism_prose: A stimulant on top of a psychedelic raises heart rate, temperature, and anxiety. The stimulant can sharpen and speed up a trip in ways that tip it toward panic or a racing, overwhelming headspace, while adding real cardiovascular load. Sleep becomes hard and the comedown is heavier.
warning_signs: Inability to sit still or calm down
first_aid: Move somewhere calm, dim, and familiar
Reassure and ground them; slow the breathing
Cool down and sip water
If chest pain or a panic state will not settle, call 911 / 112

en.mechanism_prose: A depressant blunts the body while the psychedelic keeps working on the mind. The result is often someone who looks calm but is struggling internally, or who suddenly stumbles when the depressant peaks. Alcohol adds nausea and erodes the ability to stay grounded; cannabis on a peak can tip a trip into anxiety or dissociation.
warning_signs: Stumbling or falling while seeming calm
first_aid: Move to a quiet, dim, familiar space
Reassure calmly — most difficult trips ease with grounding
Hydrate slowly; offer something light to eat
If they cannot be reached or show physical distress, call 911 / 112

en.mechanism_prose: Some research-chemical psychedelics (such as aMT) also act as MAOIs, blocking the enzyme that breaks down stimulants and serotonin. Adding a stimulant on top can drive blood pressure and body temperature to crisis levels and trigger serotonin syndrome. The result is far stronger and longer than the stimulant dose alone would suggest.
warning_signs: Severe headache, very high blood pressure
first_aid: Call 911 / 112 immediately — this can become a hypertensive or serotonin crisis
Move to a cool place and remove extra clothing
Do not give any more substances, including caffeine
Stay with them and keep 911 on the line if symptoms worsen

en.mechanism_prose: With DXM specifically, the combination is dangerous: DXM blocks serotonin reuptake, so adding a serotonergic stimulant like MDMA can trigger serotonin syndrome. DXM also has its own cardiovascular and dissociative load, and the stimulant masks how impaired the person actually is, encouraging redosing.
warning_signs: High temperature, sweating, rigidity or tremor
first_aid: Call 911 / 112 — treat as a possible serotonin-syndrome emergency
Move to a cool place; remove extra layers
Do not give more of either substance
Stay with them and keep calling 911 if symptoms worsen

en.mechanism_prose: This rating usually involves an MAOI research chemical (such as aMT) paired with a dissociative, or DXM's serotonin activity. Blocked breakdown plus serotonergic load can push toward serotonin syndrome, while the dissociative removes the awareness needed to notice things going wrong and raises injury risk.
first_aid: Call 911 / 112 — possible serotonin-syndrome emergency
Move somewhere cool, flat, and safe; reduce stimulation
Do not redose either substance
Stay with them; keep 911 on the line if symptoms worsen

en.mechanism_prose: Stacking two dissociatives compounds loss of consciousness, motor control, and protective reflexes. With longer-acting agents like DXM and PCP, a second dose lands while the first is still climbing, pushing someone into a much deeper hole than intended. Vomiting with no gag reflex makes aspiration the main killer.
warning_signs: Cannot stand, hold position, or stay awake
Breathing pauses or very shallow breathing
first_aid: Move them somewhere flat and padded; clear the airway
Place in recovery position so vomit can drain
Stay with them; do not let them get up alone
Call 911 / 112 if breathing pauses or you cannot wake them
